title: A lighthouse keeper's last year
summary: Reflect on what it means to do a job that has become unnecessary — narrating the final year of an automated lighthouse keeper — using past simple, present perfect, and a conditional.
topic: The dignity and meaning found in a job that technology made unnecessary
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) narrating the last year of a lighthouse keeper before their station was automated. What did their days involve? What did they know about the sea, the light, and the coast that a machine would not know? Reflect on whether a job can have value even when the task it was created for no longer needs a human. Use past simple, present perfect, and a conditional at least once each.
hintStarters: For thirty years, Marta had known the moods of the strait — she could tell, without looking at the forecast, that…
The machine that replaced her has never failed, as far as anyone knows, but it has also never…
If there were still someone living in the lighthouse, they might notice…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tự động hóa một quy trình
chăm sóc, duy trì ngọn đèn
còi sương, tín hiệu âm thanh trong sương mù
bị cho thôi việc do máy móc thay thế

hintVocab.phrase: the dignity of doing useful work
hintVocab.meaningVi: phẩm giá của việc làm công việc có ích
ký ức thể chế, kiến thức tích lũy trong một tổ chức

hintVocab.phrase: to sense a change in the wind
hintVocab.meaningVi: cảm nhận sự thay đổi trong gió

hintVocab.phrase: to be replaced by a machine
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị thay thế bởi máy móc
nhật ký của người trông giữ

sampleText:
Marta Svensson kept the light at Kungshamn lighthouse on the Swedish west coast for twenty-nine years. Her last year, 1999, was much like the others: she rose before dawn, checked the Fresnel lens for condensation, recorded wind speed and visibility in her log, and watched the shipping lanes through binoculars until the light shifted from grey to gold. The only thing that was different about her final year was that she knew it was her final year. The automation equipment had been installed in June. By December it would be turned on, and she would hand over the keys to a maintenance technician she would probably never see again. The log she had kept every day for nearly three decades would be the last of its kind from that station.

What Marta had built over three decades was not something the equipment could absorb. She knew that a particular drop in air pressure, combined with a south-westerly from the Danish coast, meant fog within eighteen hours — not because a manual told her so, but because she had felt it happen forty or fifty times. She knew which fishing vessel was whose by the rhythm of its engine heard across the water on still mornings. She had logged a near-grounding in 1987 when a container ship had veered a quarter-mile off the shipping lane in heavy snow; her report had resulted in the lane being remapped. The automated system that replaced her would do the core job reliably: the light would turn on at dusk and off at dawn. But it would not log the near-groundings, sense the micro-variations in the local current, or notice that the easternmost buoy had begun to drag.

I do not think Marta's work lost its value when it became technically unnecessary. If a machine had been installed in 1971 instead of Marta, the lane would not have been remapped in 1987, and the easternmost buoy might still be dragging. The argument that a job has value only while it needs a human is a clean argument, but it misses something that is harder to count: the value of having someone present, paying attention, accumulating knowledge in a way that a machine cannot because it has no stake in the place. Marta retired with a pension and a small ceremony. The log she kept for twenty-nine years is stored in a maritime archive in Gothenburg, largely unread. That seems like the wrong place for it.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect passive to describe an earlier event in a narrative
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The automation equipment had been installed in June' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành bị động (had been + quá khứ phân từ) để làm rõ rằng việc lắp đặt đã xảy ra TRƯỚC thời điểm kể chuyện (năm cuối của Marta). Đây là cách phổ biến để sắp xếp thứ tự các sự kiện trong văn tự sự.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The automation equipment had been installed in June' uses the past perfect passive (had been + past participle) to show that the installation happened BEFORE the narrative moment (Marta's final year). This is a common way to sequence events in narrative writing.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional to imagine an alternative past
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If a machine had been installed in 1971 instead of Marta, the lane would not have been remapped in 1987' dùng điều kiện loại 3 để so sánh thực tế với một lịch sử giả thuyết — đây là cách mạnh mẽ để lập luận rằng sự hiện diện của Marta đã thực sự tạo ra sự khác biệt.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If a machine had been installed in 1971 instead of Marta, the lane would not have been remapped in 1987' uses a third conditional to compare reality with a hypothetical history — a powerful way of arguing that Marta's presence actually made a difference.

sampleTranslationVi:
Marta Svensson trông giữ ngọn đèn tại ngọn hải đăng Kungshamn trên bờ biển phía tây Thụy Điển trong hai mươi chín năm. Năm cuối của bà, 1999, giống như những năm trước: bà dậy trước bình minh, kiểm tra thấu kính Fresnel để phát hiện hơi nước đọng, ghi lại tốc độ gió và tầm nhìn vào nhật ký, và quan sát các tuyến đường biển qua ống nhòm cho đến khi ánh sáng chuyển từ xám sang vàng. Điều duy nhất khác biệt trong năm cuối cùng của bà là bà biết đó là năm cuối cùng. Thiết bị tự động hóa đã được lắp đặt vào tháng Sáu. Đến tháng Mười Hai nó sẽ được bật, và bà sẽ bàn giao chìa khóa cho một kỹ thuật viên bảo trì mà bà có lẽ sẽ không bao giờ gặp lại.

Những gì Marta đã xây dựng trong ba thập kỷ không phải là thứ mà thiết bị có thể tiếp thu. Bà biết rằng một đợt giảm áp suất không khí cụ thể, kết hợp với gió tây nam từ bờ biển Đan Mạch, có nghĩa là sương mù trong vòng mười tám giờ — không phải vì sách hướng dẫn nói như vậy, mà vì bà đã cảm nhận điều đó xảy ra bốn mươi hay năm mươi lần. Bà biết tàu đánh cá nào là của ai qua nhịp điệu động cơ của nó nghe thấy qua mặt nước vào những buổi sáng yên tĩnh. Bà đã ghi chép một vụ suýt mắc cạn vào năm 1987 khi một tàu container đã lệch một phần tư dặm khỏi tuyến đường biển trong tuyết dày; báo cáo của bà đã dẫn đến việc tuyến đường được vẽ lại. Hệ thống tự động thay thế bà sẽ thực hiện công việc cốt lõi một cách đáng tin cậy: đèn sẽ bật lúc hoàng hôn và tắt lúc bình minh. Nhưng nó sẽ không ghi chép các vụ suýt mắc cạn, cảm nhận những biến động vi mô trong dòng chảy địa phương, hay nhận thấy rằng phao ở cực đông đã bắt đầu bị trôi.

Tôi không nghĩ rằng công việc của Marta mất đi giá trị khi nó trở nên không cần thiết về mặt kỹ thuật. Nếu một máy móc đã được lắp đặt vào năm 1971 thay vì Marta, tuyến đường sẽ không được vẽ lại vào năm 1987, và phao ở cực đông có thể vẫn đang bị trôi. Lập luận rằng một công việc chỉ có giá trị khi nó cần con người là một lập luận gọn gàng, nhưng nó bỏ lỡ thứ gì đó khó đếm hơn: giá trị của việc có ai đó hiện diện, chú ý, tích lũy kiến thức theo cách mà máy móc không thể vì nó không có lợi ích gì với nơi này. Marta nghỉ hưu với một khoản lương hưu và một buổi lễ nhỏ. Cuốn nhật ký bà giữ trong hai mươi chín năm được lưu trữ trong một kho lưu trữ hàng hải ở Gothenburg, phần lớn chưa được đọc. Đó có vẻ như là nơi sai cho nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect passive to describe an earlier event?
options: The equipment had been installed before she left.
The equipment had installed before she left.
The equipment has been installed before she left.
The equipment was installed before she had left.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect passive = had been + past participle. 'Had installed' is active past perfect — the equipment cannot install itself. 'Has been installed' is present perfect, which clashes with 'she left'. The last option sequences the events oddly — 'was installed' (simple past) followed by 'had left' (past perfect) reverses the logical order.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect passive = had + been + past participle. Check active vs passive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a third conditional to imagine an alternative past?
options: If a machine had been installed earlier, the lane would not have been remapped.
If a machine was installed earlier, the lane would not have been remapped.
If a machine had been installed earlier, the lane would not be remapped.
If a machine has been installed earlier, the lane would not have been remapped.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: if + had been + past participle, + would have + past participle. 'Was installed' is simple past (second conditional). 'Would not be remapped' omits 'have'. 'Has been installed' is present perfect, not past perfect.
mcQuestions.hint: Third conditional = if + past perfect, + would + have + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'know' in the past tense to describe knowledge built through experience?
options: She knew which vessel was whose by the rhythm of its engine.
She knowed which vessel was whose by the rhythm of its engine.
She has known which vessel was whose by the rhythm of its engine.
She was knowing which vessel was whose by the rhythm of its engine.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Knew' is the correct past simple of the irregular verb 'know'. 'Knowed' is not a standard form. 'Has known' is present perfect — inconsistent with the past narrative. 'Was knowing' is incorrect — 'know' is a stative verb and is not normally used in continuous forms.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Know' is irregular — check its past simple form, and remember it is not used in continuous tenses.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes a job becoming no longer needed?
options: Her job became technically unnecessary.
Her job became technically unneeded.
Her job became technically redundant.
Her job became technically needless.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Technically redundant' is the standard phrase for a job that no longer requires a human because the function is performed by technology. 'Unnecessary' and 'needless' have the right meaning but are less standard in employment contexts. 'Unneeded' is not idiomatic.
mcQuestions.hint: In employment contexts, there is a specific adjective for a job that technology has made superfluous.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'have no stake in' to express a lack of personal investment?
options: A machine has no stake in the place it monitors.
A machine has no stake on the place it monitors.
A machine has no interest stake in the place it monitors.
A machine has not stake in the place it monitors.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have a stake in' is the fixed expression for having a personal interest or investment in something. The preposition is 'in', not 'on'. 'No interest stake' creates a non-standard compound. 'Has not stake' is ungrammatical — 'no' is the correct negative determiner here.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Have a stake' uses a fixed preposition. 'No' negates a noun; 'not' negates a verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'by + time expression' correctly to describe when something would happen?
options: By December, the light would be turned over to the automated system.
Until December, the light would be turned over to the automated system.
By the December, the light would be turned over to the automated system.
By December, the light would be turned over the automated system.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'By + month name' (no article) describes a deadline. 'Until' means up to and including that point, not 'before it'. 'The December' incorrectly uses a definite article with a month name. 'Turned over the system' omits the preposition 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'By' + month name (no article) sets a deadline. Check whether 'to' is needed with 'hand over'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'log' as a verb to describe recording observations?
options: She logged a near-grounding in the shipping lane.
She logged about a near-grounding in the shipping lane.
She logging a near-grounding in the shipping lane.
She has been logged a near-grounding in the shipping lane.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Log' as a transitive verb takes a direct object with no preposition. 'Logged about' adds an unnecessary preposition. 'Logging' is a present participle without an auxiliary. 'Has been logged' is passive — she cannot be passively logged.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Log something' is transitive — it takes a direct object with no preposition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes knowledge accumulated over a lifetime of observation?
options: decades of accumulated knowledge about the local sea
decades of accumulated knowing about the local sea
decades of accumulative knowledge about the local sea
decades of accumulating knowledges about the local sea
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Accumulated knowledge' (past participle as adjective) is standard. 'Accumulated knowing' uses a gerund instead of a noun. 'Accumulative knowledge' is less standard than 'accumulated'. 'Knowledge' is uncountable — 'knowledges' is non-standard.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Knowledge' is uncountable. The past participle 'accumulated' is more natural than 'accumulative' here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'bear witness to' correctly?
options: She had borne witness to thirty years of weather patterns in that strait.
She had beared witness to thirty years of weather patterns in that strait.
She had bore witness to thirty years of weather patterns in that strait.
She had born witness to thirty years of weather patterns in that strait.
mcQuestions.explanation: The past participle of 'bear' is 'borne' (not 'beared', 'bore', or 'born'). 'Bore' is the past simple. 'Born' is a different word (birth). 'Beared' is not a standard past participle.
mcQuestions.hint: The verb 'bear' has an irregular past participle. 'Born' is a different word entirely.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'much like' correctly to introduce a comparison?
options: Her last year was much like the others: routine, careful, and close to the sea.
Her last year was much as the others: routine, careful, and close to the sea.
Her last year was much similar to the others: routine, careful, and close to the sea.
Her last year was much the same the others: routine, careful, and close to the sea.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Much like' is the natural comparator when you want to say 'very similar to'. 'Much as' is used in a different structure ('much as I admire her…'). 'Much similar to' creates a double modifier. 'Much the same the others' omits the preposition 'as'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Much like' means 'very similar to'. Check which comparator forms natural English here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'largely' as an adverb to qualify a past participle?
options: The log is stored in an archive, largely unread.
The log is stored in an archive, mostly unread.
The log is stored in an archive, broad unread.
The log is stored in an archive, large unread.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Largely unread' is a natural adverb + past participle combination meaning 'for the most part not read'. 'Mostly unread' is also grammatically correct but slightly less formal. 'Broad' is an adjective, not an adverb — 'broadly' would be needed. 'Large' is an adjective, not an adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: Adverbs, not adjectives, modify past participles. Which option uses the correct adverb form?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marta's knowledge is described as something a machine cannot absorb. Is there a type of knowledge in your own field of work or study that you think resists automation — not because the task is too complex technically, but because it requires presence, judgment, or personal investment? What would be lost if that knowledge disappeared?
